Tafseer:
Research into the word 'Asr':
'Wa' (By) is for an oath, and 'Asr' means time. Our teacher (may Allah have mercy on him) explained this word in his commentary, and we present the essential part here. He wrote:
'"Asr" means time. Just as "dahr" refers to time as a whole, "asr" refers to the passing and speed of time; hence, its primary usage is for past time. A classical poet wrote:
"What joy is there for those who lived in past times [al-asri-l-khali]!"
Another poet wrote:
"That was a time [asrun] when I used to ride a strong and beautiful camel."'
After explaining its usage in classical Arabic, our teacher presented the summary:
'This shows that the word "Asr" on one hand recalls the states and events of past times, and on the other hand draws attention to its specific characteristic of passing rapidly. These two realities present two important results: first, Allah's decisions regarding human beings will be executed in accordance with their deeds; second, we must benefit from time—whose most prominent characteristic is its rapid passing—with utmost dedication.'
Now, reflect on why an oath is sworn by time here. The answer is: through this oath, on one hand, Allah Almighty draws attention to those historical events of retribution that occurred in this world, as documented in the Qur'an and other heavenly scriptures. On the other hand, He warns people not to spend their lives in heedlessness, but to strive to benefit from time. In exchange for these rapid moments, they can attain an eternal kingdom if they recognize their value; and if they do not, they should remember that these moments will become an eternal curse for them.
Our teacher explained this in his commentary:
'The decisions executed upon past nations were the exact recompense for their deeds. When they performed virtues, God elevated them; and when they chose transgression and corruption, His law destroyed them after granting them respite to establish the proof. To remind of this law of retribution, He swore by time here—showing that people must remember that one Day they must face this law of retribution.'
'Furthermore, a subtle point is implied: man's primary asset in this world is time, and nothing exceeds it in its rapid passing. How foolish it is for man, despite knowing this transience of time, to rely on it, remaining heedless of the transience of life, the account of the Hereafter, and the law of recompense.'
Our teacher explained this through a metaphor:
'Man's example is like a merchant who trades in ice. Instead of selling it quickly to secure his profit, he keeps it and amuses himself with its shine and coldness. Obviously, such an unwise merchant will soon have to regret his heedlessness.'
Regarding this, he pointed to another aspect:
'Furthermore, the rapid passing of time contains a glad tiding and a source of patience. That is: within this short period, if a person wishes, he can accumulate an eternal treasury of reward. An unfortunate person, infatuated with the temporary pleasures of this life, deprives himself of eternal success; but a wise person, within these few days—whose reality is like a dream or a flash of lightning—attains the eternal crown of God's pleasure and love by enduring the trials of piety and self-restraint.'

The core claim: This is the core claim proved by the oaths of time. When the value of life's respite is such that in exchange for it, man can attain an eternal kingdom, and if ignored, it becomes an eternal curse; and when time is dissolving into the past with every second beyond human control; then all humans are in extreme loss if they remain heedless of this rapidly wasting asset. Thus, presenting this as testimony, He stated: indeed, mankind is in loss, except those who believe and do righteous deeds.

This reveals that the entire value of life lies in faith and righteous deeds. The Creator granted life solely so that man would live it in accordance with His pleasure contrary to the temptations of Satan, receiving the status of 'pleased and pleasing' and the ticket to Paradise. Just as the reward of eternal Paradise in return for a few days of trial is not an ordinary reward, similarly, losing it by falling into the net of Satan is not an ordinary deprivation.
The meaning of faith: The definition of faith has appeared in various places. Briefly, believing in God with all His attributes and their requirements with a sincere heart is faith. Our teacher explained its meaning:
'The root of faith (iman) is "amn" (safety/peace). In Arabic, "amanahu" means: he gave him safety. In the Qur'an, He states:
(And made them safe from fear) (Quraysh, 106:4).
"Amana lahu" means: he declared him truthful and relied on him, and "amana bihi" means: he believed in him.'
'These usages exist in the Qur'an. Among His names is "Al-Mu'min" (the Giver of peace), because He grants safety to those who seek His refuge.'
'This is an ancient religious term... Thus, the certainty that is accompanied by fear of God, trust, and all requirements of belief is faith, and the person who believes in Allah, His signs, and His commands, submits himself to Him, and remains pleased with His decisions, is a believer.'
Righteous deeds: Following faith, righteous deeds are its essential requirement. When true faith is generated, it will inevitably illuminate a person's outer deeds just as it illuminates his inner self. If faith does not produce righteous deeds, it means faith has not taken root in the heart. Faith and deeds must remain in harmony. Our teacher explained this:
'The mention of righteous deeds after faith in the Qur'an serves as an explanation... Just as obeying the Messenger is connected to obeying Allah as an explanation... This explanation was necessary because some aspects of important terms remain hidden. In faith, this explanation is necessary because its place is the heart and intellect, where a person can deceive others and even himself. He may assume himself to be a believer while he is not. Therefore, two witnesses were appointed for faith: words and deeds. Since words can be false, verbal confession alone is not sufficient, but deeds must confirm faith. (Footnote 1) Thus, He stated:
(O you who have believed, believe...) (An-Nisa, 4:136),
meaning: O you who believe with your tongues, believe with your deeds.'
Why righteous deeds are described as 'salihat': Our teacher explained the wisdom of describing righteous deeds as 'salihat' (reforming/wholesome):
'Allah Almighty described righteous deeds as "salihat". This term points to the great wisdom that all physical, spiritual, religious, worldly, personal, and collective development of man depends on these deeds. That is: a righteous deed is one that serves as a source of life and development for man, enabling him to reach the high levels of maturity endowed within his nature.'
Further on, he wrote:
'This can be understood as: man is a part of the vast machinery of the universe. Therefore, his deeds can be righteous only when they align with the wisdom and plan Allah has appointed for the entire system. Allah did not create this world as a plaything, but established a specific system of wisdom, desiring that everything in it should occur in accordance with this system, not contrary to it.'
Society's right on man: 'And advised one another to truth and advised one another to patience' (wa tawasaw bi-l-haqqi wa tawasaw bi-s-sabr) - While the preceding part related to man's individual life, man is a social creature by nature, existing as a member of a family and a society. If he lives contrary to this, he deviates from his nature. His relationship with his family and society is natural. Just as he obtains support from them for his material life, he obtains guidance from them for his moral and spiritual development. This establishes society's right on him to care for its reform; otherwise, it is contrary to his nobility. Nature requires those who know the straight path of faith and righteous deeds to advise others to the truth, and also to advise one another to patience and firmness, because choosing the truth, standing firm upon it, and calling to it require patience and resolve.
A subtle point: Note that He did not state: 'they call to faith and righteous deeds,' but: 'they advise one another to truth and patience'. This style encompasses what was stated in the first part, while adding highly important aspects. 'Truth' (haqq) encompasses faith because it is God's right and the greatest right. Similarly, righteous deeds are related to the rights of God or His creation; hence, they are also included. This reveals that they fulfill these rights themselves, advise others to do so, and also teach the wisdom that fulfilling rights is not easy, requiring patience and resolve. Those who lack this quality will find fulfilling rights highly difficult.
The meaning of truth: Our teacher explained the meaning of 'haqq' (truth):
'"Haqq" literally means a present and established entity, but in usage, it has assumed different meanings, including:
1. An event whose occurrence is certain.
2. A statement that is accepted by intellect.
3. A duty that is morally obligatory.'
'These usages are supported by the Qur'an. Its specific meaning regarding helping the poor and weak is derived from this general meaning. To the Arabs, the greatest right is helping the weak, which is obligatory on the powerful and deserved by the needy, and is accepted by intellect and recognized by all good people. This is why goodness is described as "ma'ruf" (the recognized)—namely, something known and recognized by everyone as an established law. Thus, taking "haqq" in the meaning of helping the poor encompasses all the aforementioned meanings.'
Analysis of patience: Our teacher wrote regarding 'sabr' (patience):
'To the Arabs, "sabr" does not mean helplessness or weakness, which is the way of the helpless; rather, they regard it as the foundation of all strength and firmness. It is used repeatedly in classical Arabic, and its usages support this. Hatim at-Ta'i wrote:
"How many terrifying rivers of death we faced, over which were the bridges of swords,"
"We showed firmness [sabarna] against their difficulties with our swords, until they grew cold."' 
Citing other classical verses, he explained 'sabr' from the Qur'an:
'The Qur'an itself has clarified the meaning of "sabr":
(And those who are patient in poverty and hardship and during battle) (Al-Baqarah, 2:177).'
'This verse mentions three occasions of patience: poverty, illness, and war. Pondering over them, these are the sources of all calamities, and those who stand firm in these three trials are the patient.'
Explaining the relationship between truth and patience, he wrote:
'In summary: "truth" opens the doors of all goodness, and "patience" closes the doors of all evil; or: "truth" is the beloved objective, and "patience" is the eagerness and resolve to pursue it.'
'It is clear that after attaining success, the primary thing is standing firm upon it. Note how these two words—truth and patience—encompass all success and virtues, and what a deep relationship exists between them.'
'Indeed, several branches emerge from the same root. Faith is the root and center, and righteous deeds are its explanation. Similarly, since the heart and mind love "truth", and their maturity depends on it, their love produces "patience". Love requires standing firm for the beloved. This firmness is in accordance with the status of the beloved. The more beloved something is, the more resolve is shown for it. The feelings of defense and honor are not identical for everything, but vary in accordance with love. The more beloved something is to the heart, the more jealousy and honor are aroused for it.'
'Allah's wrath and retribution are also because He loves "truth"; hence, His wrath is aroused against those who violate it. If something is beloved to you, will you tolerate its humiliation? Your honor will be aroused for its defense. A mother loves her child, and this love is accompanied by a jealous honor that makes her sacrifice her life to defend her child. The same is the case with nations defending their rights. Even a weak dove possesses this love and honor for her eggs; if you try to take them, she will defend them with her weak wings. This shows that patience is indeed produced by the love of truth.'
Answering another important question: What is the relationship between 'faith and righteous deeds' and 'advising one another to truth and patience'? Our teacher answered:
'"And advised one another to truth and advised one another to patience" shows that they possess the qualities of truth and patience themselves, and they call others to them. This is implied within the verse, because "those who believe and do righteous deeds" made this clear. Furthermore, the bad nature of advising without practicing is so obvious that it cannot be assumed that they would advise others while lacking these qualities themselves.'
'This shows that just as faith produced righteous deeds, righteous deeds produced "advising". When "truth" becomes beloved to a person, and he is ready to endure difficulties for its sake, his knowledge, love, and honor for it will increase. He will not only desire to love it himself, but will desire that the entire world loves it; and whenever he sees the truth oppressed and falsehood dominant, he will be grieved and will urge others to defend the truth. His urging others is also the natural result of his own love and honor for the truth. Thus, "advising" is mentioned here as a part and explanation of "righteous deeds".'
_____
Footnote 1: It should noted that the discussion here is about true faith, not its legal or jurisprudential aspect. Those who wish to understand the legal aspect may refer to its relevant place. Our teacher pointed to some aspects in his commentary on Surah Al-Asr, and we have also explained some aspects in this book.
